summary: Joel Spolsky argues that Microsoft's practice of sending free laptops to bloggers is ethically indistinguishable from bribery, even when bloggers disclose the gifts. He critiques Robert Scoble's defense that disclosure makes the practice ethical, pointing out that gifts create a social obligation of reciprocity as described in Cialdini's 'Influence.' Spolsky acknowledges his own imperfect record on accepting vendor gifts but announces he will no longer accept anything from vendors going forward. He argues the practice damages credibility for all bloggers, not just those who accept gifts, and models his new approach on Consumer Reports' policy of buying everything at retail.
key_points: Microsoft and its PR agency Edelman sent free high-end laptops to bloggers with Vista preinstalled, creating a subtle obligation to write positive coverage
Disclosure alone doesn't solve the ethical problem because gifts create unconscious social obligations of reciprocity that influence coverage regardless
The practice of gifting bloggers 'pisses in the well' by reducing credibility for all blogs, including those written by bloggers who don't accept gifts
Scoble's argument that something is ethical because it's someone's job is logically fallacious
Spolsky announces a new personal policy of accepting nothing from vendors and buying all products at retail, modeled on Consumer Reports
money_quotes: Just because it's someones job to do something, doesn't make it ethical.
I've been thinking long and hard about this, and the only conclusion I can come to is that this is ethically indistinguishable from bribery.
Even if no quid-pro-quo is formally required, the gift creates a social obligation of reciprocity.
This is the most frustrating thing about the practice of giving bloggers free stuff: it pisses in the well, reducing the credibility of all blogs.
I'm upset that people trust me less because of the behavior of other bloggers.
Who are the most trusted reviewers out there? Consumer Reports, probably. They don't take anything from vendors.
I'd have to live in a monastery to completely avoid those kinds of conflicts of interest.
By reading this article you are contributing, at least indirectly, to the Joel Gets a Hot Tub fund.
key_insight: Vendor gifts to bloggers are functionally equivalent to bribery because they create unconscious obligations of reciprocity that corrupt the medium, regardless of whether the blogger discloses the gift.
